mnemonics.title: Process States Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: NEW READY RUNNING WAITING TERMINATED - REMEMBER THE SEQUENCE AS 'NEVER READY TO WORK' (N=New, R=Ready, T=Terminated, W=Waiting, W=Waiting - actually use: NEW = Not Created, READY = Ready, RUNNING = Running, WAITING = Waiting, TERMINATED = Finished)

mnemonics.title: PCB Components
mnemonics.content: PCB HOLDS 'CSI' - CPU registers, State, and Information (PID, Priority, Program Counter, Memory pointers, Accounting data)

acronyms.usage: Data structure storing all process information maintained by the OS kernel
CPU register pointing to the next instruction to execute
The main processor that executes process instructions
Operations involving data transfer between CPU and peripheral devices

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a CIRCULAR PATH: NEW enters at left, moves RIGHT to READY, goes UP to RUNNING, from RUNNING either goes DOWN to READY (preemption) or LEFT to WAITING (I/O), from WAITING returns RIGHT to READY, from RUNNING goes DOWN to TERMINATED (exit)
Think of a LAYERED CAKE: Top layer is STACK (grows down), middle is HEAP (grows up), bottom is DATA segment (initialized variables), base is TEXT segment (code). Each layer has specific purpose.

Imagine an OLYMPIC RELAY: Each runner (process) carries a baton (PCB with state). When passing, one runner's info is recorded (save) and another's info is loaded (restore) - the baton transfer time is context switch overhead.

analogies.analogy: A PROCESS IS LIKE A RUNNING RESTAURANT: The recipe book is the PROGRAM (static instructions), while the actual cooking execution is the PROCESS (active, changing state). Multiple cooks can follow the same recipe (program) simultaneously creating different dishes (processes).

PCB IS LIKE A STUDENT'S ACADEMIC RECORD: Contains identification (PID), current status (state), courses enrolled (memory info), grades (accounting), and attendance (resource usage) - everything needed to track that student's progress.

CONTEXT SWITCH IS LIKE A TEACHER SWITCHING BETWEEN STUDENTS: Teacher stops helping one student, writes down where they left off, picks up another student's work from where they left off. The time spent switching is overhead not contributing to learning (execution).
